entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 3

classification.title: Fish and Game Law
classification.summary: This document appears to be the cover page or introductory section of the Fish and Game Laws of the State of Kentucky, in effect July 1, 1912. It lists the Fish and Game Commission and its Executive Agent, J. Quincy Ward.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a title page for the Fish and Game Laws of Kentucky from 1912. It does not contain any substantive legal provisions, nor does it mention race or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it cannot be classified as a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it refers to laws in effect from a specific date, typical of legislative acts passed during a session.

entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 5

classification.title: AN ACT FOR THE PROTECTION OF GAME AND FISH
classification.summary: This act creates a Game and Fish Commission with perpetual succession, corporate powers, and the ability to make bylaws and rules. The commission will consist of four members who serve without compensation, except for expenses. The act also outlines the appointment process for these commissioners, specifying terms and party affiliations.
classification.reasoning: The statute establishes a Game and Fish Commission and outlines its structure, appointment process, and powers. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'Act' passed by the General Assembly. Confidence is high because the content is clearly about wildlife management and not racial matters.

entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 6

classification.title: Fish and Game Law 1912
classification.summary: This statute outlines the appointment, powers, and duties of the Game and Fish Commission. It details the appointment process for commissioners, their terms, and how vacancies are filled. The commission is tasked with enforcing laws related to the protection, preservation, and propagation of game birds, game animals, fish, songbirds, and insectivorous birds and animals. It also specifies the commission's responsibilities in managing these resources for public value.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the structure and responsibilities of a Game and Fish Commission. It focuses on the appointment of commissioners, their terms, and their duties related to wildlife and fish management. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The statute appears to be purely administrative and regulatory concerning natural resources. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 7

classification.title: Fish and Game law 1912
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers and duties of the Game and Fish Commission, including collecting and distributing game, seizing illegally taken game and related instrumentalities, and managing state hatcheries and preserves. It also mandates a biennial report to the Governor.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the functions of a Game and Fish Commission, focusing on wildlife management, conservation, and reporting. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 8

classification.title: Fish and Game Law 1912
classification.summary: This section of the 1912 Fish and Game Law details reporting requirements for the Game and Fish Commission, including the number of hunter's licenses issued, fees collected, and financial information. It also outlines the appointment and duties of an Executive Agent, who is responsible for managing the commission's affairs when it is not in session, and requires a bond for faithful performance.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the administrative and financial operations of the Game and Fish Commission and the role of an Executive Agent. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 9

classification.title: Fish and Game law 1912
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers and responsibilities of the Game and Fish Commissioners in Kentucky, including their ability to appoint and remove game wardens, possess and transport fish, birds, and animals for conservation purposes, and acquire land and water for wildlife protection and propagation. It also details the process for condemning land if an agreement on value cannot be reached.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to the regulation of fish and game in Kentucky. It details the administrative powers of the Game and Fish Commissioners, their ability to appoint wardens, and their authority to acquire and manage land for conservation purposes. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Fish and Game law 1912
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers and responsibilities of a Fish and Game Commission, including the acquisition of land and water for fish hatcheries and game preserves, the employment of personnel, and the appropriation of funds from the Fish and Game Protection Fund. It details procedures for land acquisition through purchase, lease, or condemnation, and allows for the sale of land and water with the Governor's consent.

It focuses on the establishment and maintenance of fish hatcheries and game preserves, land acquisition, and fund management. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Fish and Game Law 1912
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers and duties of the Fish and Game Commission, including the sale and reinvestment of proceeds from real estate or water used for conservation purposes. It also details the authority of wardens and other peace officers to enter into contracts for the preservation of fish, birds, and animals, and to arrest individuals violating these laws.

classification.title: Fish and Game law 1912
classification.summary: This statute declares any fish, game, or animal caught, killed, shipped, or possessed contrary to the act's provisions as contraband. It outlines the seizure and search of such contraband and directs its delivery to the Commission of Fish and Game for disposal. It also details the process for apprehending offenders and bringing them before a court or magistrate for trial.

The content focuses on the regulation of fishing and hunting, the seizure of illegal game, and the legal process for offenders. entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 13

classification.title: Fish and Game Law 1912
classification.summary: This statute outlines procedures for the seizure and search of illegally caught, taken, killed, possessed, or shipped fish or game. It grants courts the authority to issue search warrants for suspected contraband and to break open receptacles. Officers are required to report seizures to the Game and Fish Commission. It also defines and addresses contraband devices used in violation of fish and game laws.

classification.title: Fish and Game law 1912
classification.summary: This statute declares certain structures on ice or water as public nuisances and authorizes their abatement. It also outlines the sale of contraband fish and game seized under the act, with proceeds split between the seizing officer and the State Treasury.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details regulations concerning fish and game, including the declaration of nuisances and the sale of seized contraband. entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 15

classification.title: Fish and Game Law 1912
classification.summary: This section of the Fish and Game Law of 1912 outlines regulations for the sale and resale of fish, game, game animals, and birds. It mandates that the Commission keep records of sales, tag or mark items before selling, and destroy seized contraband. It also prohibits purchasers from reselling or further disposing of these items, and prevents any other person from buying or possessing them other than the original purchaser from the Commission. Additionally, it assigns enforcement duties to various law enforcement officials and allows the Commission to employ legal counsel.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to the regulation of fish and game sales and enforcement of related laws. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The regulations apply universally to all individuals involved in the sale and purchase of fish and game. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Fish and Game Law 1912
classification.summary: This section of the Fish and Game Law of 1912 outlines the legal services the Commission can require, including representation in civil actions and prosecution of criminal actions related to fish and game laws. It also details the powers of executive agents, commission members, and appointed wardens in serving and executing warrants and processes, and their authority to call for assistance from law enforcement officers.

classification.title: Fish and Game Law 1912
classification.summary: This section of the Fish and Game Law of 1912 outlines the process for obtaining hunting licenses. It details the information required for a resident's license, including age, residence, physical characteristics, and eye/hair color. It also mentions the printing of blanks and other necessary materials for the act and game laws, to be paid for from the Game and Fish Protection Fund.

entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 19

classification.title: Fish and Game law 1912
classification.summary: This section of the 1912 Fish and Game law outlines the requirements for obtaining hunting licenses for both residents and non-residents/aliens in Kentucky. It details the information needed for an affidavit, the cost of the licenses, and the process for county clerks to issue them. For non-residents or aliens, it specifies a higher fee and the need for written permission from the landowner.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the process and fees for obtaining hunting licenses for residents and non-residents/aliens. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The distinction is between residency status and alien status, not race. entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 20

classification.title: Fish and Game law 1912
classification.summary: This section of the 1912 Fish and Game law outlines the procedures for issuing hunting licenses, including the fees retained by county clerks and the remittance of the balance to the State Treasurer for the Game and Fish Protection Fund. It also states that resident owners, landowners, and their family members, as well as tenants and their family members, may hunt on their own land or leasehold without a license. Additionally, it specifies that resident and non-resident licenses will be printed in distinctive colors and details the delivery and return process of licenses to county clerks.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to the administration of hunting licenses and exemptions for landowners and tenants. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 21

classification.title: Fish and Game law 1912
classification.summary: This section of the 1912 Fish and Game law details the process for county clerks to return unused hunting licenses and stubs to the Commission. It also establishes the Game and Fish Protection Fund, which is comprised of moneys from hunting licenses, contraband, fines, penalties, and forfeitures. This fund is designated for the payment of the Game and Fish Commission's expenses, including salaries and travel expenses for wardens, executive agents, office assistants, and other employees. The State's liability for these expenses is limited to the amount available in the Game and Fish Protection Fund.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the administrative procedures for hunting licenses and the creation and management of a Game and Fish Protection Fund. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It also mentions a Game and Fish Protection Fund.

classification.title: Fish and Game law 1912
classification.summary: This section of the Fish and Game law from 1912 outlines exceptions to hunting license requirements for individuals hunting on their own or adjacent lands. It also details the taxation of prosecution costs and the distribution of fines and penalties collected for violations of fish and game laws. A portion of the fines is allocated to the Executive Agent, Warden, or other appointed officers involved in the apprehension and conviction of offenders.

entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 24

classification.title: Fish and Game law 1912
classification.summary: This section of the 1912 Fish and Game law details the disposition of fines, penalties, and forfeitures. It states that all moneys collected from these sources, relating to fish or game laws, shall belong to the State Game and Fish Protection Fund, with exceptions for payments to executive agents, wardens, and public officers. These collected funds are to be paid to the State Treasurer monthly and credited to the fund. The State Treasurer is required to report the exact amount to the Game and Fish Commission monthly. It also specifies that all fines must be paid in lawful currency of the United States. Finally, it mandates that circuit judges give special charges to Grand Juries regarding game and fish laws.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from a 1912 Fish and Game law. It outlines the financial procedures for fines and penalties related to fish and game regulations, including how the money is collected, distributed, and reported. entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 25

classification.title: Fish and Game law 1912
classification.summary: This section of the 1912 Fish and Game law details the financial reporting and appropriation for the Game and Fish Commission. It outlines the requirement for the Executive Agent to submit monthly itemized expense accounts for approval by the Commission, which then authorizes the Auditor of Public Accounts to draw warrants on the State Treasurer from the Game and Fish Protection Fund. It also appropriates $5,000 for the first year's expenses, covering all costs including salaries and operational expenses, to be repaid to the State from the Game and Fish Protection Fund.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the financial management and operational funding of the Game and Fish Commission. entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 31

classification.title: Fish and Game Law 1912
classification.summary: This statute prohibits the catching, killing, or possession of quail, partridge, or pheasant during certain times. It also forbids the destruction of nests or eggs of wild birds, with exceptions for predatory species. Penalties for violations include fines. Possession of protected animals or birds within prohibited times is considered prima facie evidence of unlawful taking, and selling them during these times carries the same penalty as unlawful killing.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to regulations concerning the hunting and possession of specific game birds and the protection of wild bird nests and eggs. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute focuses solely on wildlife conservation and related offenses. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 32

classification.title: Fish and Game Law 1912
classification.summary: This statute outlines penalties for the unlawful killing, catching, or possession of protected animals and birds. It specifies that each offense is separate and punishable, and allows for the joinder of multiple offenses in a single warrant or indictment. Fines are imposed for each offense, with 50% of the fine going to the informer. It also details the powers of magistrates and judges to issue warrants for the arrest of individuals violating the law, particularly those temporarily within their jurisdiction or whose identity is unknown, and to issue search warrants for concealed game.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to fish and game laws, outlining offenses, punishments, and enforcement mechanisms. entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 33

classification.title: Fish and Game Law 1912
classification.summary: This section of the 1912 Fish and Game Law outlines the duties of city mayors, market masters, and police officers in enforcing the chapter's provisions. It mandates that these officials diligently search for and arrest individuals violating the chapter by unlawfully possessing game or offering it for sale during prohibited periods. Officers failing to perform these duties are subject to fines for misfeasance in office. The text also includes a provision making it unlawful to kill, purchase, or possess any wild bird other than a game bird, or its plumage.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to the enforcement of fish and game laws in Kentucky. It details the responsibilities of various officials in apprehending individuals who violate these laws, particularly concerning the possession or sale of game during prohibited times. It also includes a clause about the unlawful killing or possession of wild birds. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 34

classification.title: Fish and Game Law 1912
classification.summary: This statute outlines regulations for the protection of wild birds and game in Kentucky. It prohibits the sale or transportation of certain wild birds after they have been killed or caught, and restricts the sale or possession of bird plumage. It defines which birds are considered game birds and makes it unlawful to destroy the nests or eggs of any wild bird other than a game bird. Penalties for violating these provisions include fines and/or imprisonment.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to fish and game laws, specifically regulating the hunting, possession, and sale of wild birds and their parts. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that suggest differential treatment based on race. The penalties and regulations apply to all individuals within the state. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

The act repeals any inconsistent prior laws.

classification.title: Fish and Game Law 1912
classification.summary: This statute prohibits the use of nets or other contrivances that prevent fish from passing in running waters, and also prohibits the use of poisonous drugs or bait to catch fish. Penalties are outlined for violations.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses regulations related to fishing practices, specifically prohibiting certain types of nets and the use of poison in waterways. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 41

classification.title: Fish and Game law 1912
classification.summary: This section of the 1912 Fish and Game law prohibits the use of dynamite or explosive agents to catch or injure fish in the waters of Kentucky. It outlines penalties for violations, including fines and potential jail time or labor on public works for failure to pay fines. It also specifies that circuit courts and justices of the peace have jurisdiction over these offenses and that judges must charge grand juries concerning this act.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details regulations concerning the use of explosives for fishing and the associated legal penalties and jurisdictions. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is classified as 'criminal_laws' due to its focus on prohibited actions and penalties. The confidence is high because the content is clearly unrelated to racial segregation or discrimination.

entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 42

classification.title: Fish and Game Law 1912
classification.summary: This statute prohibits the use of seines, nets (other than dip nets), drags, or traps for fishing in running waters, lakes, or ponds, except for private ponds and streams forming a boundary line with other states. It also prohibits the use of drugs, injurious substances, medicated bait, dynamite, or other explosives in designated running waters with the intent to harm or catch fish. Penalties are outlined for violations, with a portion of fines going to the county treasury and the remainder to the civil officer responsible for apprehension and conviction. The circuit courts have jurisdiction over these offenses.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details regulations concerning fishing methods and the prohibition of harmful substances in waterways. It outlines penalties and jurisdiction for violations. There is no mention of race, separate facilities, differential treatment based on race, or any other indicators commonly associated with Jim Crow laws. Therefore, this statute is classified as 'no' for being a Jim Crow law.

entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 43

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 1896 of the Kentucky Statutes, relating to the destruction of fish by guns loaded with steel balls and copper jackets.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 1896 of the Kentucky Statutes concerning the destruction of fish. It specifically addresses the use of dynamiting or other explosive agents to kill fish, outlining penalties for such actions. It also includes provisions about minnow seines and trot-lines for catching fish for bait.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text is from the 1912 Fish and Game Law. It details regulations regarding fishing methods, specifically prohibiting the use of dynamiting or explosive agents to kill fish and outlining penalties. It also includes provisions for catching minnows for bait. There is no mention of race, nor any implicit or explicit racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about fishing regulations and lacks any racial component.

entries.citation: Fish and Game Laws of State of Kentucky, 1912, p. 44

classification.title: Fish and Game Law 1912
classification.summary: This statute prohibits the use of dynamite or explosive agents, or shooting into waters with ammunition other than ordinary leaden balls, with the intent to kill, injure, or catch fish. Violators are subject to fines and potential jail time or labor upon failure to pay.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses regulations for fishing practices and the penalties for violating them. It does not contain any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is classified as 'criminal_laws' because it outlines prohibited actions and their associated penalties.
